FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data with 10,574 early adolescents at T1 and 9,273 at T2

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures weighted screen time (hours/day) as specific variable; also includes sleep and physical activity in 24-hour movement framework

FT_IC3.evidence: Multivariable linear mixed-effects models test associations between screen time and multiple non-SM outcomes (cognition, BMI, brain volumes, psychosocial health)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported in tables and text; β coefficients and p-values presented for screen time associations with outcomes

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis with both cross-sectional (T1) and longitudinal (T1→T2) designs using linear mixed models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2023; original peer-reviewed empirical work

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is used as independent variable (IV) predicting cognitive, physical, and behavioral outcomes, not as dependent variable. Analysis is cross-sectional and longitudinal predictive design.
notes: Paper uses integrated 24-hour movement framework with sleep, screen time, and activity. Screen time is measured as specific continuous variable (weighted hours/day) and has separately extractable coefficients.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study); year 2 n=10,335, year 3 n=6,095

FT_IC2.evidence: Primary exposure is cyberbullying victimization. Paper does not measure or analyze specific smartphone/social media variables (texting, app use, SM time, etc.). Outcome is substance initiation, not phone/SM outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: While paper reports logistic regression tests (cyberbullying→substance initiation), there is no statistical test involving a smartphone/SM variable as predictor or outcome

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results (OR, CI, p-values) are reported but only for cyberbullying-substance associations, not for any smartphone/SM variables

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis with cross-sectional (year 2) and longitudinal (year 2→year 3) multivariable logistic regressions

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 2023; original peer-reviewed empirical work

FT_EC7_reason: Fails FT_IC2 (no phone/SM exposure variable); does not qualify as smartphone/SM screening study
notes: This paper is about cyberbullying-substance initiation associations in ABCD. While cyberbullying may be online-mediated, the paper does not isolate or analyze specific smartphone/social media variables (texting, SM platforms, app use, etc.). Falls under exclusion per FT-IC2 NOT_MET.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data; 5,027 early adolescents assessed pre-pandemic and across six timepoints (May 2020-March 2021)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen time activities: social media use and video gaming (recreational screen time). Both are smartphone/SM-qualifying exposures.

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effect models test associations between social media use and video gaming with sleep outcomes (time in bed, bedtimes, sleep onset latency)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values reported; associations between screen time variables and sleep behaviors with statistical significance and effect estimates

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis using mixed-effect longitudinal models examining pre-pandemic baseline and pandemic timepoints

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Health Psychology, 2023; original peer-reviewed empirical work

FT_EC7_reason: Social media and video gaming are independent variables predicting sleep outcomes, not dependent variables. Cross-sectional and longitudinal predictive design with behavioral outcomes.
notes: Paper explicitly examines recreational screen time (social media and video gaming) as predictors of sleep behaviors during COVID-19 pandemic. Screen time variables are separately measured and analyzed as independent variables.
